romania.summary: Unul dintre cele mai mari fonduri de private equity din Europa Centrală și de Est, Enterprise Investors, a finalizat luni, la București, investiția de până la 20,4 milioane de euro în lanțul românesc de săli de fitness 18GYM, după obținerea aprobărilor necesare.

romania.title: Șapte persoane rănite la Călărași după ce o pasarelă a cedat în timpul unui eveniment pe Dunăre
romania.summary: Șapte persoane, cu vârste între 2 și 33 de ani, au fost rănite în municipiul Călărași, după ce o pasarelă de îmbarcare a cedat. Victimele au suferit răni ușoare și au fost transportate la spital pentru investigații medicale. Polițiștii au deschis o anchetă pentru a stabili circumstanțele în care s-a produs incidentul.

Potrivit declarațiilor sale, pentru fiecare campionat pierdut de echipă, Becali va construi câte o biserică ortodoxă, continuând astfel tradiția ctitoririi de lăcașuri sfinte ca formă de recunoștință și rugăciune. Noul produs, denumit „Catedrala Neamului – Ediție Aurită”, include peste 5.000 de piese placate cu aur, replici detaliate ale turlelor, mozaicurilor și elementelor arhitecturale emblematice ale catedralei naționale românești. romania.summary: Un studiu recent realizat de specialişti în antropologie culturală şi ştiinţe comportamentale scoate în evidenţă persistenţa unui obicei ancestral pe litoralul românesc al Mării Negre. Femeile din România manifestă o predilecţie marcată pentru îngroparea partenerilor lor în nisipul plajei, un gest interpretat ca o expresie a legăturilor profunde şi a ritualurilor de reconectare cu natura. medical.title: Scans reveal lithium distribution in bipolar disorder
medical.summary: A revolutionary scanning technique has revealed that lithium MRI is a powerful tool for studying how the drug interacts with the brain and could offer more personalized treatment for bipolar disorder.

medical.title: Fish-inspired sensor tracks how human heart tissue responds to disease and treatment
medical.summary: Engineers have developed a new way to monitor how tiny lab-grown human heart tissues beat—by effectively "listening" to the ripples they create. The team has created a wireless, noninvasive sensing platform that can biomechanically measure how strongly the miniature heart tissues, known as cardiac organoids, beat in real time. The research could help accelerate drug development, improve disease modeling and reduce reliance on animal testing, offering a more human-relevant way to study how the heart works.

medical.title: How the brain's physical shape guides its internal wiring
medical.summary: A new study led by Monash University researchers has shed light on the factors shaping the intricate wiring of our brains. The research, published in the journal Cell, reveals that the brain's complex wiring diagram, known as the cortical connectome, does not form at random. Instead, a new mathematical model shows that connections preferentially form between locations that support natural, shape-driven "resonant patterns."

medical.title: People with disabilities often overlooked in orthopedic research
medical.summary: More than 1 in 4 adults in the United States have some type of disability as of 2026, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. A Yale study published in the Cureus found that patients with disabilities are almost entirely unrepresented in orthopedic research.

medical.title: New therapeutic target for cancer identified by revealing how cancer 'hijacks' blueprint for blood vessel development
medical.summary: Anti-angiogenic therapies targeting VEGF have been widely used in cancer treatment, yet their long-term efficacy remains limited. Tumor vascular endothelial cells (TECs) exhibit high adaptive plasticity, enabling them to resist treatment and sustain tumor growth, but the molecular mechanism underlying this plasticity has remained poorly understood.

medical.title: Two treatments help avoidant restrictive food intake disorder, a common pediatric eating disorder, trial shows
medical.summary: The first randomized, controlled trial for a common childhood eating disorder has shown how therapists and parents can help those struggling with the condition, known as avoidant restrictive food intake disorder, or ARFID.

medical.title: Supporting people with disability to participate in sport
medical.summary: Taking part in sport offers more than just improved fitness or mental well-being for people with disability; it can also build confidence, independence and create a deeper sense of belonging in the community. Despite the well-known benefits, participation in sport by individuals with disability remains low. Around 5.5 million people live with a disability in Australia, but only 14% participate in sport at least once a week, compared with about 67% of Australians without disability.

medical.title: What's the buzz about mosquitoes this year?
medical.summary: With mosquito populations peaking well above the 10-year average in Saskatchewan and double last year's count at this time, the buzz across Saskatchewan is how bad it will get, and what we can do to help enjoy our time outside.

medical.title: Popular GLP-1 weight loss drugs are being misused by people struggling with eating disorders, research reveals
medical.summary: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs), such as Ozempic or Wegovy, have revolutionized type 2 diabetes and weight-loss management, but they have also led to some unfortunate outcomes. A recent study in JAMA Psychiatry has raised concerns that GLP-1 medications may be misused by people with eating disorders. These are serious mental health conditions in which thoughts about food, body weight and body shape become overwhelming, leading to harmful eating behaviors and an intense drive to control food intake. Although concerns about weight or appearance are common, eating disorders go far beyond these everyday worries.

medical.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
medical.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

medical.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

medical.title: Scientists discover hidden “footprints of death” that may help viruses spread
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new twist in what happens when cells die. As dying cells break apart, they leave behind tiny “footprints of death” packed with newly discovered particles that help guide the immune system to clean up the remains. But researchers found that influenza viruses can exploit this process, hiding inside these microscopic packages and potentially using them to spread to nearby cells.

medical.title: Study challenges a common belief about vitamin D and sunlight
medical.summary: A study of nearly 300 people across northern Britain found that vitamin D levels often stay low all year in groups most at risk. Surprisingly, summer sunshine did not significantly boost vitamin D levels among older adults or people from minoritized ethnic backgrounds.

medical.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
medical.summary: Eating five servings of fruits and vegetables may not be enough if you're missing foods rich in flavanols, a group of compounds linked to better heart health. Researchers found that choices like blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea can dramatically increase flavanol intake.

medical.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer’s
medical.summary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.

science.title: Why people worldwide see some mental abilities as inborn and others as learned
science.summary: When does a child begin to reason? When do they develop self-control? Are some mental abilities present from birth, while others are acquired through experience? Questions like these have fascinated philosophers, educators and scientists for centuries. Yet surprisingly little is known about how ordinary people think about the development of the mind itself. Do people across cultures think about the mind in similar ways?

science.title: For hiring, remote work means more expertise, research finds
science.summary: Remote work during the COVID-19 pandemic may have relaxed company expectations concerning employees' business attire. But it raised expectations for employees' qualifications, work experience, skills and educational credentials when hiring, according to new research from Northeastern University.

science.title: Light-activated compound kills antibiotic-resistant bacteria by turning its own defense enzyme against it
science.summary: Antibiotic resistance is becoming an accelerating crisis because of the overuse and misuse of antibiotics over many years. The problem is exacerbated when antibiotics wipe out susceptible bacteria but leave resistant bacteria behind to multiply, further spreading resistance. There is an ongoing search for new treatments to fight resistant bacteria, and now researchers may have found a way to successfully treat at least one type of resistant bacteria.

science.title: Inviting students to shape support systems can improve mental health and campus environments
science.summary: By listening to students and partnering with them to shape solutions, Dr. Adam Alvarez's research shows that schools can build trust, reduce isolation and create supportive communities where students feel valued.

science.title: Giraffes combine quantities similarly to addition
science.summary: In addition to humans, some species of primates and birds have demonstrated under experimental conditions their ability to manipulate quantities in tasks that require combining or separating them, in a manner similar to addition or subtraction. Now, a study by the University of Barcelona, published in the journal Scientific Reports, reveals that some giraffes are also capable of deciding which of two options contains more food after mentally combining two quantities, a process comparable to simple addition. These results suggest that giraffes possess a cognitive foundation that could support more complex arithmetic skills.

science.title: Tiny DNA 'hitchhikers' may be reshaping life in thawing Arctic soils
science.summary: Amid the peatlands of northern Sweden, billions of microbes are quietly rewriting their genetic playbooks—and doing so far more often than scientists realized.

science.title: Europa's ice shell secrets unlocked by ground radar study
science.summary: Jupiter's moon Europa has become high-value real estate for astrobiologists and the search for life beyond Earth. This is because the small moon, which is slightly smaller than Earth's moon, boasts a massive subsurface ocean of liquid water that scientists estimate contains about double the amount of water in all Earth's oceans combined. As seen on Earth, water equals life, so scientists are eager to continue exploring Europa in any way possible to determine if it could harbor life as we know it, or even as we don't know it.

science.title: NASA Astronaut Chris Williams Preps for Spacewalk
science.summary: Flight engineer Sophie Adenot of ESA (European Space Agency) helps flight engineer Chris Williams of NASA as he tries on his spacesuit on June 23, 2026, testing its comfort and mobility as well as its communications and life support systems inside the International Space Station’s Quest airlock. Williams will go on a spacewalk on June […]

science.title: Mapping Earth’s Observations, featuring Betsy Ford
science.summary: NASA’s Earth-observing satellites track an enormous range of phenomena: how aerosols move through the atmosphere, how moisture descends through soil, how land-cover shifts over decades. It’s some of the most consequential data NASA produces, informing science, policy, agriculture, and climate research around the world. As NASA’s Earth Science Division (ESD) manages this vast portfolio, they […]

science.title: Functional NIN persists in non-nodulating plants: Rethinking the loss of symbiosis
science.summary: Certain plants, including legumes, form specialized root organs known as nodules. These plants establish symbiotic relationships with nitrogen-fixing bacteria and utilize atmospheric nitrogen. This process, termed "root nodule symbiosis," is an important biological function that can reduce reliance on chemical nitrogen fertilizers. At the genetic level, previous studies, primarily on legumes, have demonstrated that the transcription factor nodule inception (NIN) is essential for root nodule symbiosis. This finding has led to the prevailing view that the loss of the NIN gene is primarily responsible for nodulation loss.

science.title: Childbirth is not uniquely difficult to humans
science.summary: The tight fit of a baby's head through a mother's birth canal, which causes great difficulty in childbirth, is not unique to humans, as previously understood. Instead, some small-bodied primate babies have heads almost twice as large as their mothers' pelvic space, a new study led by UCL researchers has found.

science.title: NextSTEP-3 A: Lunar Enabling Technology
science.summary: NASA issued a draft Broad Agency Announcement under NextSTEP‑3, Appendix A, on June 29, 2026, to advance concepts that accelerate the technological readiness of critical systems for lunar surface and cislunar architecture. This solicitation seeks to close key technology gaps and mature capabilities in vertical solar arrays, ISRU oxygen production systems, Stirling radioisotope generators, in‑space […]

science.title: Exposure to violence contributes to high rates of suicidal thoughts and attempts among transgender people
science.summary: A new analysis from the Williams Institute at UCLA School of Law of the 2022 U.S. Transgender Survey data finds that among respondents ages 16 and older, 39% reported serious suicidal thoughts in the year before the survey and 5% had attempted suicide. In comparison, the 2022 National Survey of Drug Use and Health reports that 5% of adults in the U.S. general population have considered suicide and 0.6% have attempted it.

science.title: Online calculator shows how drastically mowing affects insects
science.summary: How many insects and spiders live in 1 square meter (11 square feet) of meadow? What impact do humans have on this biodiversity in mowed meadows, lawns and roadside verges? A new online tool answers these questions: the Insect Calculator, developed by researchers at TU Darmstadt.

science.title: Age bias discovered in AI
science.summary: Do responses generated by artificial intelligence systems such as ChatGPT reflect social prejudice? A KAIST research team has quantitatively analyzed and identified age-related stereotypes embedded in the responses of generative artificial intelligence. The study sheds light on the potential impact of hidden AI biases on social perceptions and suggests directions for the development of more inclusive AI.

science.title: Red-tailed hawks maintain flight performance despite missing feathers
science.summary: Red-tailed hawks can compensate for feather loss during molt by subtly changing their wing and tail movements, according to a new study by University of California, Davis, researchers in the College of Engineering and the Weill School of Veterinary Medicine.

science.title: DNA databases unite to create a fully open resource for transposable element research
science.summary: For more than three decades, researchers studying genomes have relied on foundational resources such as Repbase and, more recently, Dfam to identify and classify transposable elements—the mobile DNA sequences that shape genome structure, evolution and function. Now, Dfam and Repbase are coming together under a single, fully open framework.

science.title: Nanopore technology identifies proteins molecule by molecule
science.summary: Proteins are responsible for most functions in the human body. However, their analysis, which is essential for understanding diseases, developing drugs and discovering new biomarkers, remains highly complex. Using a technology called "nanopore detection," a team at the University of Geneva (UNIGE) has developed a rapid and efficient method for identifying proteins, molecule by molecule. These findings, published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, pave the way for faster diagnostics.

science.title: Why nanoscale droplets don't coalesce and microscale droplets do
science.summary: Olive oil and water do not naturally mix. Water molecules are polar, having a net electric dipole moment due to the bend angle of about 104.5° between the two oxygen-hydrogen bonds. Olive oil is nonpolar due to its long hydrocarbon chains, which makes it hydrophobic and insoluble in water. Mixtures of the two are called emulsions, and emulsifiers exist that can stabilize them into a thicker temporary or permanent mixture. Cooks use such a technique to make vinaigrette, a salad dressing consisting primarily of oil and vinegar with emulsifiers such as mustard, honey or mayonnaise.

science.title: Why South Africa’s leopards shrank to half their normal size
science.summary: A hidden population of South African leopards has revealed a remarkable evolutionary story. Researchers analyzing entire leopard genomes discovered that the Cape Floristic Region’s leopards are not only much smaller than most African leopards, but also genetically distinct after being isolated for roughly 20,000 years. Surprisingly, despite their small population, they have retained much of their genetic diversity.

science.title: Scientists expected a black hole but found a neutrino factory powered by stars
science.summary: A distant galaxy nicknamed Shadow Blaster may have revealed a surprising source of cosmic neutrinos: extreme star formation instead of a supermassive black hole. The discovery suggests that hidden, dust-filled starburst galaxies could account for a significant fraction of the Universe’s high-energy neutrinos.

science.title: Researchers found a Wordle strategy that wins 99% of the time
science.summary: Researchers developed a Wordle-solving strategy that succeeds 99% of the time by focusing on information gain rather than likely answers. The method uses Shannon entropy to identify guesses that reveal the most about the hidden word. Each guess is designed to slash uncertainty and narrow the possibilities faster. The result significantly outperformed more traditional Wordle tactics.

science.title: Einstein’s “biggest blunder” may finally have an explanation
science.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising connection between quantum gravity and an exotic quantum state of matter that could explain why the universe isn’t expanding wildly fast. The study suggests that the very shape of space-time may protect the cosmological constant from disruptive quantum effects.

science.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry’s surprising origins
science.summary: Researchers have created a new way to reconstruct the evolutionary history of complex plant genomes by analyzing genetic traces left by transposable elements. The technique revealed that modern strawberries were assembled through multiple ancient genome-merging events, shedding new light on how major crop species evolved.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Sport Club Municipal Timișoara pune orașul în mișcare la Ziua Timișoarei | Timișoara City Celebration
mix_items.summary: Sport Club Municipal Timișoara aduce sportul în centrul orașului și transformă zona Uszoda și Bulevardul Nicolae Titulescu în cea mai amplă zonă dedicată sportului și mișcării în aer liber din… Articolul Sport Club Municipal Timișoara pune orașul în mișcare la Ziua Timișoarei | Timișoara City Celebration apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: O mai bună diferențiere a detaliilor clinice prin imagini bogate în contrast și cu zgomot de fundal redus. Pentru procedurile coronariene, un nou protocol cu doză ultra-scăzută utilizează o doză… Articolul Inovație în cardiologie: Philips lansează tehnologia care reduce la jumătate doza de radiație X în timpul intervențiilor pe inimă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Șapte persoane rănite la Călărași după ce o pasarelă a cedat în timpul unui eveniment pe Dunăre
mix_items.summary: Șapte persoane, cu vârste între 2 și 33 de ani, au fost rănite în municipiul Călărași, după ce o pasarelă de îmbarcare a cedat. Victimele au suferit răni ușoare și au fost transportate la spital pentru investigații medicale. Polițiștii au deschis o anchetă pentru a stabili circumstanțele în care s-a produs incidentul.

mix_items.title: De ce investește un fond polonez 20,4 milioane EUR într-o rețeaua românească de săli de fitness
mix_items.summary: Unul dintre cele mai mari fonduri de private equity din Europa Centrală și de Est, Enterprise Investors, a finalizat luni, la București, investiția de până la 20,4 milioane de euro în lanțul românesc de săli de fitness 18GYM, după obținerea aprobărilor necesare.

mix_items.title: Gala aniversară Crucea Roșie Română – 150 de ani de activitate umanitară | Ateneul Român
mix_items.summary: Crucea Roșie Română marchează 150 de ani de activitate umanitară cu o Gală aniversară găzduită de Ateneul Român în data de 2 iulie 2026. Filmată și difuzată de Televiziunea Română,… Articolul Gala aniversară Crucea Roșie Română – 150 de ani de activitate umanitară | Ateneul Român apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Gigi Becali, cunoscutul cioban și patron al echipei FCSB, a anunțat un proiect ambițios inspirat de modelul domnitorului Ștefan cel Mare. Potrivit declarațiilor sale, pentru fiecare campionat pierdut de echipă, Becali va construi câte o biserică ortodoxă, continuând astfel tradiția ctitoririi de lăcașuri sfinte ca formă de recunoștință și rugăciune. Inițiativa vine pe fondul unei […] The post Ca Ștefan cel Mare! Becali construiește o biserică pentru fiecare campionat pe care-l pierde appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Frecventele conflicte în cuplu pot produce pe termen lung distanțare, suferință, pierderea încrederii și chiar destrămarea relației. Totuși, în opinia specialiștilor Erin şi Stephen Mitchell, nu conflictul în sine este… Articolul Autorii cărții „Conflicte care apropie”, Erin și Stephen Mitchell oferă o nouă perspectivă asupra vieții de cuplu și a conflictelor cu rol de apropiere, după apariția copiilor apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Firmele nu vor mai depune raportări contabile semestriale pentru anul 2026. Anunțul Ministerului Finanțelor
mix_items.summary: Ministerul Finanţelor elimină, pentru acest an, obligaţia operatorilor economici de a întocmi şi depune raportări contabile semestriale la 30 iunie.

mix_items.title: 😂 Lego lansează primul set cu piese de aur, ca să-ți construiești propria Catedrală a Neamului
mix_items.summary: Compania daneză Lego a anunțat lansarea primului său set special cu piese de aur, conceput special pentru construirea unei replici a Catedralei Neamului. Noul produs, denumit „Catedrala Neamului – Ediție Aurită”, include peste 5.000 de piese placate cu aur, replici detaliate ale turlelor, mozaicurilor și elementelor arhitecturale emblematice ale catedralei naționale românești. Potrivit reprezentanților Lego, […] The post Lego lansează primul set cu piese de aur, ca să-ți construiești propria Catedrală a Neamului appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Obiceiuri ancestrale. Femeile adoră să-și îngroape bărbatul în nisip pe plajă
mix_items.summary: Un studiu recent realizat de specialişti în antropologie culturală şi ştiinţe comportamentale scoate în evidenţă persistenţa unui obicei ancestral pe litoralul românesc al Mării Negre. Femeile din România manifestă o predilecţie marcată pentru îngroparea partenerilor lor în nisipul plajei, un gest interpretat ca o expresie a legăturilor profunde şi a ritualurilor de reconectare cu natura. […] The post Obiceiuri ancestrale. Femeile adoră să-și îngroape bărbatul în nisip pe plajă appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Leapmotor B05 BEV, primul vehicul electric sportiv al mărcii, îmbină performanțele dinamice cu tehnologia avansată și o experiență de utilizare intuitivă. Prin dotările orientate către șofer,...

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Why people worldwide see some mental abilities as inborn and others as learned
mix_items.summary: When does a child begin to reason? When do they develop self-control? Are some mental abilities present from birth, while others are acquired through experience? Questions like these have fascinated philosophers, educators and scientists for centuries. Yet surprisingly little is known about how ordinary people think about the development of the mind itself. Do people across cultures think about the mind in similar ways?

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: For hiring, remote work means more expertise, research finds
mix_items.summary: Remote work during the COVID-19 pandemic may have relaxed company expectations concerning employees' business attire. But it raised expectations for employees' qualifications, work experience, skills and educational credentials when hiring, according to new research from Northeastern University.

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... mix_items.title: Light-activated compound kills antibiotic-resistant bacteria by turning its own defense enzyme against it
mix_items.summary: Antibiotic resistance is becoming an accelerating crisis because of the overuse and misuse of antibiotics over many years. The problem is exacerbated when antibiotics wipe out susceptible bacteria but leave resistant bacteria behind to multiply, further spreading resistance. There is an ongoing search for new treatments to fight resistant bacteria, and now researchers may have found a way to successfully treat at least one type of resistant bacteria.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

La SOS Satele Copiilor România, oferim o familie copiilor ajunși... The post Contabil appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Scans reveal lithium distribution in bipolar disorder
mix_items.summary: A revolutionary scanning technique has revealed that lithium MRI is a powerful tool for studying how the drug interacts with the brain and could offer more personalized treatment for bipolar disorder.

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Inviting students to shape support systems can improve mental health and campus environments
mix_items.summary: By listening to students and partnering with them to shape solutions, Dr. Adam Alvarez's research shows that schools can build trust, reduce isolation and create supportive communities where students feel valued.

mix_items.title: Fish-inspired sensor tracks how human heart tissue responds to disease and treatment
mix_items.summary: Engineers have developed a new way to monitor how tiny lab-grown human heart tissues beat—by effectively "listening" to the ripples they create. The team has created a wireless, noninvasive sensing platform that can biomechanically measure how strongly the miniature heart tissues, known as cardiac organoids, beat in real time. The research could help accelerate drug development, improve disease modeling and reduce reliance on animal testing, offering a more human-relevant way to study how the heart works.

mix_items.title: Giraffes combine quantities similarly to addition
mix_items.summary: In addition to humans, some species of primates and birds have demonstrated under experimental conditions their ability to manipulate quantities in tasks that require combining or separating them, in a manner similar to addition or subtraction. Now, a study by the University of Barcelona, published in the journal Scientific Reports, reveals that some giraffes are also capable of deciding which of two options contains more food after mentally combining two quantities, a process comparable to simple addition. These results suggest that giraffes possess a cognitive foundation that could support more complex arithmetic skills.

mix_items.title: Tiny DNA 'hitchhikers' may be reshaping life in thawing Arctic soils
mix_items.summary: Amid the peatlands of northern Sweden, billions of microbes are quietly rewriting their genetic playbooks—and doing so far more often than scientists realized.

mix_items.title: How the brain's physical shape guides its internal wiring
mix_items.summary: A new study led by Monash University researchers has shed light on the factors shaping the intricate wiring of our brains. The research, published in the journal Cell, reveals that the brain's complex wiring diagram, known as the cortical connectome, does not form at random. Instead, a new mathematical model shows that connections preferentially form between locations that support natural, shape-driven "resonant patterns."

mix_items.title: People with disabilities often overlooked in orthopedic research
mix_items.summary: More than 1 in 4 adults in the United States have some type of disability as of 2026, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. A Yale study published in the Cureus found that patients with disabilities are almost entirely unrepresented in orthopedic research.

mix_items.title: New therapeutic target for cancer identified by revealing how cancer 'hijacks' blueprint for blood vessel development
mix_items.summary: Anti-angiogenic therapies targeting VEGF have been widely used in cancer treatment, yet their long-term efficacy remains limited. Tumor vascular endothelial cells (TECs) exhibit high adaptive plasticity, enabling them to resist treatment and sustain tumor growth, but the molecular mechanism underlying this plasticity has remained poorly understood.

mix_items.title: Europa's ice shell secrets unlocked by ground radar study
mix_items.summary: Jupiter's moon Europa has become high-value real estate for astrobiologists and the search for life beyond Earth. This is because the small moon, which is slightly smaller than Earth's moon, boasts a massive subsurface ocean of liquid water that scientists estimate contains about double the amount of water in all Earth's oceans combined. As seen on Earth, water equals life, so scientists are eager to continue exploring Europa in any way possible to determine if it could harbor life as we know it, or even as we don't know it.

mix_items.title: NASA Astronaut Chris Williams Preps for Spacewalk
mix_items.summary: Flight engineer Sophie Adenot of ESA (European Space Agency) helps flight engineer Chris Williams of NASA as he tries on his spacesuit on June 23, 2026, testing its comfort and mobility as well as its communications and life support systems inside the International Space Station’s Quest airlock. Williams will go on a spacewalk on June […]

mix_items.title: Mapping Earth’s Observations, featuring Betsy Ford
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Earth-observing satellites track an enormous range of phenomena: how aerosols move through the atmosphere, how moisture descends through soil, how land-cover shifts over decades. It’s some of the most consequential data NASA produces, informing science, policy, agriculture, and climate research around the world. As NASA’s Earth Science Division (ESD) manages this vast portfolio, they […]

mix_items.title: Functional NIN persists in non-nodulating plants: Rethinking the loss of symbiosis
mix_items.summary: Certain plants, including legumes, form specialized root organs known as nodules. These plants establish symbiotic relationships with nitrogen-fixing bacteria and utilize atmospheric nitrogen. This process, termed "root nodule symbiosis," is an important biological function that can reduce reliance on chemical nitrogen fertilizers. At the genetic level, previous studies, primarily on legumes, have demonstrated that the transcription factor nodule inception (NIN) is essential for root nodule symbiosis. This finding has led to the prevailing view that the loss of the NIN gene is primarily responsible for nodulation loss.

mix_items.title: Childbirth is not uniquely difficult to humans
mix_items.summary: The tight fit of a baby's head through a mother's birth canal, which causes great difficulty in childbirth, is not unique to humans, as previously understood. Instead, some small-bodied primate babies have heads almost twice as large as their mothers' pelvic space, a new study led by UCL researchers has found.

mix_items.title: NextSTEP-3 A: Lunar Enabling Technology
mix_items.summary: NASA issued a draft Broad Agency Announcement under NextSTEP‑3, Appendix A, on June 29, 2026, to advance concepts that accelerate the technological readiness of critical systems for lunar surface and cislunar architecture. This solicitation seeks to close key technology gaps and mature capabilities in vertical solar arrays, ISRU oxygen production systems, Stirling radioisotope generators, in‑space […]

mix_items.title: Exposure to violence contributes to high rates of suicidal thoughts and attempts among transgender people
mix_items.summary: A new analysis from the Williams Institute at UCLA School of Law of the 2022 U.S. Transgender Survey data finds that among respondents ages 16 and older, 39% reported serious suicidal thoughts in the year before the survey and 5% had attempted suicide. In comparison, the 2022 National Survey of Drug Use and Health reports that 5% of adults in the U.S. general population have considered suicide and 0.6% have attempted it.

mix_items.title: Two treatments help avoidant restrictive food intake disorder, a common pediatric eating disorder, trial shows
mix_items.summary: The first randomized, controlled trial for a common childhood eating disorder has shown how therapists and parents can help those struggling with the condition, known as avoidant restrictive food intake disorder, or ARFID.

mix_items.title: Supporting people with disability to participate in sport
mix_items.summary: Taking part in sport offers more than just improved fitness or mental well-being for people with disability; it can also build confidence, independence and create a deeper sense of belonging in the community. Despite the well-known benefits, participation in sport by individuals with disability remains low. Around 5.5 million people live with a disability in Australia, but only 14% participate in sport at least once a week, compared with about 67% of Australians without disability.

mix_items.title: Online calculator shows how drastically mowing affects insects
mix_items.summary: How many insects and spiders live in 1 square meter (11 square feet) of meadow? What impact do humans have on this biodiversity in mowed meadows, lawns and roadside verges? A new online tool answers these questions: the Insect Calculator, developed by researchers at TU Darmstadt.

mix_items.title: Age bias discovered in AI
mix_items.summary: Do responses generated by artificial intelligence systems such as ChatGPT reflect social prejudice? A KAIST research team has quantitatively analyzed and identified age-related stereotypes embedded in the responses of generative artificial intelligence. The study sheds light on the potential impact of hidden AI biases on social perceptions and suggests directions for the development of more inclusive AI.

mix_items.title: Red-tailed hawks maintain flight performance despite missing feathers
mix_items.summary: Red-tailed hawks can compensate for feather loss during molt by subtly changing their wing and tail movements, according to a new study by University of California, Davis, researchers in the College of Engineering and the Weill School of Veterinary Medicine.

mix_items.title: DNA databases unite to create a fully open resource for transposable element research
mix_items.summary: For more than three decades, researchers studying genomes have relied on foundational resources such as Repbase and, more recently, Dfam to identify and classify transposable elements—the mobile DNA sequences that shape genome structure, evolution and function. Now, Dfam and Repbase are coming together under a single, fully open framework.

mix_items.title: Nanopore technology identifies proteins molecule by molecule
mix_items.summary: Proteins are responsible for most functions in the human body. However, their analysis, which is essential for understanding diseases, developing drugs and discovering new biomarkers, remains highly complex. Using a technology called "nanopore detection," a team at the University of Geneva (UNIGE) has developed a rapid and efficient method for identifying proteins, molecule by molecule. These findings, published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, pave the way for faster diagnostics.

mix_items.title: What's the buzz about mosquitoes this year?
mix_items.summary: With mosquito populations peaking well above the 10-year average in Saskatchewan and double last year's count at this time, the buzz across Saskatchewan is how bad it will get, and what we can do to help enjoy our time outside.

mix_items.title: Popular GLP-1 weight loss drugs are being misused by people struggling with eating disorders, research reveals
mix_items.summary: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs), such as Ozempic or Wegovy, have revolutionized type 2 diabetes and weight-loss management, but they have also led to some unfortunate outcomes. A recent study in JAMA Psychiatry has raised concerns that GLP-1 medications may be misused by people with eating disorders. These are serious mental health conditions in which thoughts about food, body weight and body shape become overwhelming, leading to harmful eating behaviors and an intense drive to control food intake. Although concerns about weight or appearance are common, eating disorders go far beyond these everyday worries.

mix_items.title: Why nanoscale droplets don't coalesce and microscale droplets do
mix_items.summary: Olive oil and water do not naturally mix. Water molecules are polar, having a net electric dipole moment due to the bend angle of about 104.5° between the two oxygen-hydrogen bonds. Olive oil is nonpolar due to its long hydrocarbon chains, which makes it hydrophobic and insoluble in water. Mixtures of the two are called emulsions, and emulsifiers exist that can stabilize them into a thicker temporary or permanent mixture. Cooks use such a technique to make vinaigrette, a salad dressing consisting primarily of oil and vinegar with emulsifiers such as mustard, honey or mayonnaise.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
mix_items.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden “footprints of death” that may help viruses spread
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new twist in what happens when cells die. As dying cells break apart, they leave behind tiny “footprints of death” packed with newly discovered particles that help guide the immune system to clean up the remains. But researchers found that influenza viruses can exploit this process, hiding inside these microscopic packages and potentially using them to spread to nearby cells.

mix_items.title: Why South Africa’s leopards shrank to half their normal size
mix_items.summary: A hidden population of South African leopards has revealed a remarkable evolutionary story. Researchers analyzing entire leopard genomes discovered that the Cape Floristic Region’s leopards are not only much smaller than most African leopards, but also genetically distinct after being isolated for roughly 20,000 years. Surprisingly, despite their small population, they have retained much of their genetic diversity.

mix_items.title: Study challenges a common belief about vitamin D and sunlight
mix_items.summary: A study of nearly 300 people across northern Britain found that vitamin D levels often stay low all year in groups most at risk. Surprisingly, summer sunshine did not significantly boost vitamin D levels among older adults or people from minoritized ethnic backgrounds.

mix_items.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
mix_items.summary: Eating five servings of fruits and vegetables may not be enough if you're missing foods rich in flavanols, a group of compounds linked to better heart health. Researchers found that choices like blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea can dramatically increase flavanol intake.

mix_items.title: Scientists expected a black hole but found a neutrino factory powered by stars
mix_items.summary: A distant galaxy nicknamed Shadow Blaster may have revealed a surprising source of cosmic neutrinos: extreme star formation instead of a supermassive black hole. The discovery suggests that hidden, dust-filled starburst galaxies could account for a significant fraction of the Universe’s high-energy neutrinos.

mix_items.title: Researchers found a Wordle strategy that wins 99% of the time
mix_items.summary: Researchers developed a Wordle-solving strategy that succeeds 99% of the time by focusing on information gain rather than likely answers. The method uses Shannon entropy to identify guesses that reveal the most about the hidden word. Each guess is designed to slash uncertainty and narrow the possibilities faster. The result significantly outperformed more traditional Wordle tactics.

mix_items.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer’s
mix_items.summary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.

mix_items.title: Einstein’s “biggest blunder” may finally have an explanation
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising connection between quantum gravity and an exotic quantum state of matter that could explain why the universe isn’t expanding wildly fast. The study suggests that the very shape of space-time may protect the cosmological constant from disruptive quantum effects.

mix_items.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry’s surprising origins
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a new way to reconstruct the evolutionary history of complex plant genomes by analyzing genetic traces left by transposable elements. The technique revealed that modern strawberries were assembled through multiple ancient genome-merging events, shedding new light on how major crop species evolved.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am luat sticla de apa ca sa fiu adult responsabil. Am umplut-o cu suc.

satire_ro.title: La mulţi ani, Florin Prunea, cu ocazia zilei de Sf. Metru şi Pavel
